revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23987730427|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Matsuri-bayashi]][[ja:祭囃子]]
{{Unreferenced|date=June 9, 2015}} '''Festival Hayashi''' (祭囃子, Matsuri-bayashi) refers to the music performed during [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]] and is considered a genre of {{ill|Traditional Japanese music|en|Traditional Japanese music|qid=Q7832297|lt=hōgaku|1=Traditional Japanese music}}. It is often simply called "ohayashi" or "hayashi." However, this article focuses solely on music associated with festivals and does not cover the "hayashi" that accompanies {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|qid=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}}, {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|qid=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}, [[kagura]], or other {{ill|Performing arts|en|Performing arts|qid=Q184485|lt=Performing arts|1=Performing arts}}. == Overview == Festival hayashi is a general term for music associated with festivals, which has developed closely with the festivals of each region. Therefore, it does not refer to a uniform style of music or instrumentation across Japan, nor does it trace back to a single origin. The content of the music varies depending on the purpose, character, and scene of the festival. While festival hayashi often evokes lively, festive tunes like those in the nursery song "Muramatsuri," there are also slow and serene pieces. Some regions even refer to ensemble drumming forms, such as those using the [[fukushiki-fukudaho]] method, as "festival hayashi." The term encompasses a wide variety of music. === Performers === Unlike other hōgaku genres, the performers of festival hayashi are often {{ill|Ujiko|en|Ujiko|qid=Q125562175|lt=Ujiko|1=Ujiko}} (parishioners of a shrine) or {{ill|danka|en|danka|qid=Q5219931|lt=danka|1=Danka}} (parishioners of a Buddhist temple) from the community. While professional hōgaku performers sometimes play festival hayashi, it is rare for someone to specialize exclusively in this genre. Professionals who also perform festival hayashi include [[taikagurashi]] (Shinto ritual performers), kagura masters, hōgaku musicians, and [[Shinto priests]]. Similar to how there are no professional [[mikoshi]] carriers or {{ill|Yatai|en|Yatai|qid=Q8050151|lt=Yatai|1=Yatai}} pullers, festival hayashi is typically practiced by ujiko or danka who rehearse for the festival and perform only during the event. In some regions, the performers are exclusively children.
=== Instruments === The instruments used in festival hayashi are {{ill|Traditional Japanese musical instruments|en|Traditional Japanese musical instruments|qid=Q1071649|lt=wagakki|1=Traditional Japanese musical instruments}}. Common instruments include: * '''[[:en:Flutes|Flutes]]''': {{ill|Shinobue|en|Shinobue|qid=Q3100072|lt=Shinobue|1=Shinobue}}, [[:en:nōkan|nōkan]], {{ill|Ryūteki|en|Ryūteki|qid=Q1760249|lt=Ryūteki|1=Ryūteki}}, etc. * '''[[:en:Japanese drums|Japanese drums]]''': {{ill|Shime-daiko|en|Shime-daiko|qid=Q4148082|lt=Shime-daiko|1=Shime-daiko}} for mid-range tones, [[:en:ōdaiko|ōdaiko]] or [[ōdō]] for low-range tones, and other drums like taiko, uchiwa-daiko, and kakari-daiko. * '''{{ill|Kane (instrument)|en|Kane (instrument)|qid=Q2280552|lt=Kane|1=Kane (instrument)}}''': [[suri-gane]] (struck gongs) or [[atarigane]], used for high tones. Some regions incorporate [[:en:string instruments|string instruments]], and some performances focus solely on taiko tied to [[mikoshi]]. Additionally, chanting, {{ill|kakegoe|en|kakegoe|qid=Q6349354|lt=kakegoe|1=Kakegoe}} (shouted phrases), or [[kiryō]] ([[tekomae]]) may be included. === Performance Style and Attire === The style of performance and attire varies depending on the nature of the festival. When festivals take place within a shrine, performances are held on a noh stage, kagura hall, or a temporary stage built within the shrine grounds. During processions, performers may walk while playing or perform on a yatai or a float. Performers often wear colorful {{ill|Happi|en|Happi|qid=Q1332700|lt=Happi|1=Happi}} (traditional Japanese coats) or other flamboyant [[:en:wafuku|wafuku]] (Japanese clothing). In some regions, performers wear heavy makeup, influenced by the unique styles and professions of the performers. === Origin === The origins of festival hayashi vary by region and community. In the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}, there are theories of {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|qid=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}} origin and {{ill|Kasai|en|Kasai|qid=Q228489|lt=Kasai|1=Kasai}} origin, but neither has been definitively proven.<ref>[https://www.kyogei.co.jp/shirabe/kyoudo/text14.html Kyōgei Corporation, "Kyōdo no Ongaku: Tōkyōto"]</ref> == Types of Festival Hayashi == The following are some examples of festival hayashi from different regions in Japan: * '''[[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]''' * '''[[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]]''' ** [[Kakunodate Festival#Kazariyama Hayashi|Kazariyama Hayashi]] ** [[Hanawa-bayashi]] ** [[Hidaka Firefighting Festival|Hidaka Hayashi]] * '''{{ill|Kanto|en|Kanto|qid=Q3812869|lt=Kanto|1=Kanto}}''' ** [[Ishioka Hayashi]] ** [[Shinkawa Hayashi]] ** [[Kasai Hayashi]] ** [[Kanda Hayashi]] ** [[Edo Hayashi]] ** [[Meguro Hayashi]] ** [[Haneda Hayashi]] ** [[Chichibu Yatai Hayashi]] ** [[Shinagawa Bayashi]] ** [[Sahara Bayashi]] ** [[Shigematsu Hayashi]] ** [[Kamakura Hayashi]] ** [[Odawara Hayashi]] ** [[Nishi Mitsugi Bayashi]] ** [[Haika Bayashi]] ** [[Manazuru Hayashi]] ** [[Iwa Hayashi]] ** [[Hama-no-te-ryū]] * '''[[:en:Chubu|Chubu]]''' ** [[Sansha Festival Hayashi]] ** [[Fujinomiya Hayashi]] ** Others: Ishimatsu Hayashi, Yatai-shita Hayashi, Miyabayashi, Amadare Hayashi, Tekka Hayashi * '''[[Kinki (Kansai)]]''' ** [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Hayashi]] ** [[Ise Ondo]] ** [[Danjiri-bayashi]] * '''[[:en:Chugoku|Chugoku]]''' ** [[Kōhachimangū#Kōhachimangū Festival Hayashi|Kōhachimangū Hayashi]] * '''[[Shikoku]]''' * '''[[Kyushu]]''' ** [[Boshita Hayashi]] == References == {{Reflist}} {{Music of Japan}} {{Traditional Japanese Performing Arts}} {{event-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|祭囃子|version=100636337|comment=Imported full ja history}}
